next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 9

The motel alarm went off at 4:47, and Eli was awake before it finished the second chirp because he had never really slept. The room had that high-country dry cold that found seams in bad windows. Every time the heater kicked on it blew a smell like hot dust and old pennies. He sat on the bed for a minute with his boots unlaced and his shirt in his hands, listening to weather talk through the wall from the room next door.

A woman on the local station kept saying words like *advisory* and *deteriorating corridor* while color maps bloomed red over the mountains. The map didn’t care about scale. It made the pass look the size of a thumbnail, a quick pinch between one town and the next. He knew better. He had driven enough winters in western Pennsylvania to respect snow, but this wasn’t Pennsylvania snow. This was wind lifting entire shoulders of it and throwing it sideways at steel.

He pulled on his shirt slowly because the left side of his chest still remembered the wrenching motion from last night when he had changed the front pads in a truck stop lot with borrowed tools and a flashlight in his mouth. The repair had bought him noise reduction, not confidence. The pedal still felt soft at the end. The mechanic in Kansas had called it "serviceable if you keep your speed down and your luck up."

Luck was what people named bad math after the fact.

His burner sat on the sink with one bar left. He plugged it in while he shaved with hotel soap and looked at himself in the mirror that made everyone look jaundiced. He was fifty-two, with cheeks rough with gray, eyes bl...
open_hooks_to_carry: Inspection scene confirms brake wear he cannot properly afford to fix
Pawn shop exchange strips symbolic protection from the marriage
prev_tail_excerpt:
...to the rumble strip and jerked back. He told himself he would stop at the next rest area, then the next, then after one more song.

His phone lit up with Nora's name but no call came through, only a text.

`Need confirmation by noon tomorrow that you have a job lead with start time. No speeches.`

He could have answered honest: no lead, no start time, just highway. Instead he typed, `Working on it now. Will confirm by noon.` He hit send before he could edit the sentence into truth.

Fifteen minutes later the brake pedal gave a soft, spongy inch before it caught. The change was small, but it was there. He pumped it once at an exit ramp and felt it firm up. On the shoulder a reflective sign flashed past: `STEEP GRADES WEST OF LIMON - CHECK BRAKES.` He registered the words one by one, too late to do anything with them. The sign was in the mirror before his brain filed it under urgent.

He gripped the wheel tighter and rolled his shoulders to wake up. The atlas had slid open to Colorado on the passenger seat. Theo's penciled circles glowed pale under the dash light, each one a little target he had promised to hit and did not yet know how to survive.

At 12:37 a.m. he crossed the state line with the tank half full, forty-eight dollars in his wallet, just over eight hundred folded in the envelope under his seat, a legal deadline in his windshield, and a promise already starting to crack. The highway rose almost imperceptibly toward black country he could not see. Behind him Missouri and Kansas flattened into memory. Ahead, the grade steepened.

He did not take the next rest exit.
state_deltas_required: Prepare for mountain travel with failing brakes and too little cash.
He sells his wedding band at a pawn shop for far less than its value and commits to crossing at dawn.
